subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Legal Challenges vs. Social Movements for Civil Rights Policy Change
stimulus: In the United States, groups use different methods in order to achieve their policy goals. Take a position on whether legal challenges or social movements are the most effective means for changing federal policy in the area of civil rights.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: The Federalist 78, The First Amendment, "Letter from Birmingham Jail".)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents in the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Legal challenges are most effective because decisions of the court do not necessarily need to appeal to public opinion and have the effect of changing policy in the area of civil rights.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Social movements are most effective because large numbers of people can put pressure on the political system which has resulted in policy changes in the area of civil rights.

wrong_examples: History has shown that legal challenges are clearly the most effective in changing federal policy. (States a conclusion without a line of reasoning — no 'because' clause explaining why)
Both legal challenges and social movements are effective. (No clear position taken — must choose one or the other as 'most effective')
common_traps: The prompt requires choosing one method as 'most effective' — a thesis that says both are equally effective does not earn Row A
A line of reasoning requires at least one supporting reason — use a 'because' clause or equivalent to explain why your chosen method is most effective

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award the HIGHEST tier met. Tier 2 and 3 require Row A. Tier 3 requires at least one piece from Federalist 78, the First Amendment, or Letter from Birmingham Jail.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions Federalist 78, the First Amendment, Letter from Birmingham Jail, Brown v. Board of Education, the Civil Rights Act, or another relevant civil rights course concept.

wrong_examples: Completely off-topic evidence with no connection to civil rights or methods of policy change
common_traps: Tier 1 does NOT require Row A — even a student with no thesis can earn 1 evidence point for relevant evidence
At Tier 1, evidence only needs to be topically relevant — it does not need to directly support the thesis

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. Requires Row A.

required_elements: One specific piece that directly supports the thesis, OR two relevant pieces
alternatives.correct_example: Cites Brown v. Board of Education to show that legal challenges successfully changed federal policy on school desegregation, directly supporting the claim that legal challenges are most effective.

wrong_examples: Mentions the Letter from Birmingham Jail without explaining how it supports either position on legal challenges vs. social movements
common_traps: Tier 2 REQUIRES Row A — a student without a thesis cannot advance beyond 1 evidence point
Evidence must be specific: 'the courts are powerful' is not specific enough — cite a case, document, or concrete historical example

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, at least one from a listed foundational document (Federalist 78, First Amendment, or Letter from Birmingham Jail). Requires Row A.

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis
At least one from: Federalist 78, First Amendment, or Letter from Birmingham Jail
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Federalist 78 to argue that an independent judiciary can protect civil rights without political pressure, AND uses Brown v. Board of Education as a concrete example of a legal challenge producing lasting civil rights change — both supporting the thesis.

wrong_examples: Two pieces of evidence but neither from the listed documents — cites Brown v. Board and the Civil Rights Act without referencing Federalist 78, the First Amendment, or Letter from Birmingham Jail
Cites the Letter from Birmingham Jail but mischaracterizes it as arguing AGAINST civil disobedience (it actually argues FOR the moral obligation to resist unjust laws)
common_traps: The Letter from Birmingham Jail argues FOR civil disobedience and social movement activism — if arguing that social movements are most effective, this document directly supports the thesis
Federalist 78 argues for an independent judiciary as guardian of constitutional rights — useful for supporting the legal challenges position

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain HOW or WHY the evidence supports the claim — not just restate it.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Court cases such as Brown v. Board of Education established a precedent for desegregating schools which was one of the biggest achievements of the Civil Rights movement. Because schools are locally controlled, it would have been much more difficult to achieve such a broad victory through a social movement alone.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Political protest and civil disobedience are actions that can be taken when direct challenges to the system have been exhausted. When a social movement gains prominent recognition, legislatures are more likely to move quickly to address the concerns of their citizens, unlike a legal challenge which can be costly and slow.

wrong_examples: The evidence shows that legal challenges are effective. (Does not explain the causal chain — why does the evidence show this?)
Brown v. Board of Education was an important case. (States a fact without connecting it to the thesis)
common_traps: Reasoning must show the mechanism: why does the evidence logically lead to the conclusion that legal challenges (or social movements) are most effective?
Requires Row A AND Row B ≥ 2 pts — a student who earns these prerequisite rows but writes superficial reasoning still does not earn Row C

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut — not merely acknowledge it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Some argue that social movements are better because they mobilize a large number of people for change, but social movements require coordination and participation which takes time and can easily be co-opted or dissipate.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Some argue that legal challenges are more effective because they don't require changing public opinion, but legal challenges generally require financial support, legal expertise, and could take decades to resolve — making them inaccessible to many civil rights groups.

wrong_examples: Many would argue that legal challenges are better, but this is simply untrue. (Dismisses the alternate perspective without engaging with it substantively)
Some would argue that social movements are better because they mobilize people for change. (Only identifies the alternate perspective — no rebuttal)
common_traps: The rebuttal must engage substantively with the alternate view — not just say it is wrong; explain why your position is stronger or acknowledge a limitation of the opposing view
A concession + rebuttal is fully acceptable: 'While legal challenges have limitations like cost and time, they produce binding rulings that states cannot simply ignore'
